comment: Hempel schließt sich der Deutung Wernberg-Mollers (und damit van der Ploegs) an, dass es sich hierbei um einen aramäischen Infinitv handelt, und verweist zudem auf Qimrons Vorschlag, hier ein Verbalnomen zu sehen (Qimron HDSS, 65). Zudem verweist sie auf die Gemeinsamkeit von dieser Form in 1QS und 4Q257 3,2, was es unwahrscheinlicher werden lässt, dass es sich hier um korrupten Text handelt (Alexander and Vermes DJD 26).

comment: Metso schreibt in der Anmerkung: "It is unclear whether the scibe intended to write מעון (dwelling) or מעין (spring). The stroke of yod seems quite long to support the reading of מעין, but, paralleled with מקור in this context, מעין seems more suited. Prov 25:26 similarly parallels מעין and מקור. See also 1QS 10:12, where vav/yod is similarly questionable."

comment: Metso schreibt in der Anmerkung: "It is unclear whether the scribe first wrote אל נקמות (as in Ps 94:1) with taw as the last letter but then secondarily erased the vav preceding it with the intention of reading the last letter as a he, i.e, נקמה. A small speck of the left tick of the vav is clearly visible, as is the bottom of the vertical stroke. Alternatively, the intended text was אל נקמות, and the scribe made no erasure, but rather the surface of the leather later slit, effectively erasing the vav and posing the question of the interpretation of the last letter."

comment: Entre ויבש et וצישו, les traces sont clairement celles d'un עגזו, mot qui devrait avoir une signification florale. Le mot est inconnu en hébreu, et les autres dictionnaires ne nous aident pas à expliquer la racine (vien que les dictionnaires arabes nous fourbissent leurs délices habituelles: je note au passage " ´i`jazat<un>, a thing ressembling a pillow with which a woman enlarges he hinder part, binding it upon that part, in order that she may be thought to be large in her hinder part when she is not so."). Mais l'explication la plus simple est que le ע a été biffé (il est en effet très endommagé) et ainsi nous aurons גזו "his mowed grass" ce qui s'accorde avec le context et le parallélisme. Comme autre solution possible, Mgr P.W. Skehan suggère une métathèse de גזו , voir Isaie 40,21.

comment:
Ce mot de J.M. Allegro est difficile, mais peut-être pas impossible. Très hypothétiquement nous suggérons
ולא ד֯ג֯ל עליה (dalet douteux et gimel mutilé)- voir notre tarduction - en faisant une coupe différente entre les traces; mais ce dalet est difficile-il ressemble, comme d'autres dalet de ce manuscrit, plutôt à un resh.

comment: Notes on reading: Sometimes the word טוהר defindes the blended colours of the woven work....but based on the term "angels of fire" (Z.4) זוהר is preferable. Noteworthy are the parallel meanings of זהר and טהר in Ugaritic writings. Such parallelism is possibly reflected in Ex 24,10, and in the scribe's error (4Q403 1,42) זו}טוהר}.

comment:
Notes on reading: ט֯וביך על כו֯ל .This phrase may either refer to God or to Israel, depending on the meaning of טוביך . The definition is either "your bounty"(טובך ) , with the syllable before the pronoun ך- being spelled with yod and accented( Qimron, HDSS, 33-5), or "your good men". For the first meaning cf. Ps 31:20; Neh 9:25, 35, where the Lord`s bounty to Israel is specified. In this case, its connection with על כול may reflect a play on טוב ה לכל ורחמיו על כל מעשיו "The Lord is good to all, and His mercy is over all His works" (Ps 145:9). The author vocalizes the adjective טוב "good" as the noun טוב "bounty", rendering the first stiche as "the bounty of the Lord is of all" and then combines it with על כל from the second stiche, yielding "Your bounty is over all". However, the appeal to God should be explained in the light of lines 8, 10, and 14. As for the meaning "good men", טובים appears in Biblical Hebrew (Ps 125:4; Prov 14:19; 15:3, etc.), referring to those men considered faithful to God. Yet, טובים with a pronoun is possibly reflected in the LXX version of Zech 6:10, 14, and see the Talmudic term טובי העיר (b. Meg. 26a). Its meaning here could be "Your best men" referring to the men of Israel, see lines 7-10 below, and the metaphor עץ טוב in frg. 2 ii 3. Its connection with על כול is understood if the author is referring both to Ps 145:9-10, modifying חסידיך of v. 10 into the synonym טוביך , and continuing with על כול from v. 9. In this case the whole phrase may have been
מודים טוביך על כול (das מודי müsste in Klammern stehen) "Your good men(give thank)s for all (...", directed to Israel.

comment: As a biblical term it is the name of one of Japhet's desecnedents (Gen 10:4; 1 Chr 1:5, 7). Biblical prophets used the term as a general designation of the Greeks (Isa 66:19; Ezek 27:13, 19; Zech 9:13). In the Book of Daniel the term is applied to Alexander the Great and the Hellenistic kingdoms (8:21; 10:20; 11:2). The few Qumran occurrences of the term designate more specifically the Seleucid kings (CD VIII 11; XIX 24; 4QpNah [4Q169] 3-4 i 2). this may indeed be the meaning here. In this context it should be noted htat APOCRYPHON OF JEREMIAH C appears to reserve the appellation יון for the Seleucid kingdom, whereas the Ptolemaic kingdom is referred to as 'Egypt'.

comment: Given the general context this form is better parsed as a 3rd fem. sing. imperfect (תִּכָּבֵש), rather than a 2nd masc. sing. of the Nip'al. It would then apply to Egypt or a neigbouring country. The verb כבש means in the bible 'to subdue' and 'to press, trample', both of which fit the context of war and conquest.

comment: The form should be parsed as a noun, as is suggested by the definite article. בתר. means 'a cut piece' (cf. Gen 15:10; Jer 34:19). A derivative meaning occurs in Song 2:17 הרי בתר, 'cleft mountains', which is perhaps to be restored here (הרי] הבתר) as the place where the Tree of Life stands. Cf. COMMENTS to col. ii 2-3 and analysis.

comment: Notes on reading: last letter ambigous: ו or י. If מבואו is read, the sing. suffix could refer to God...or the the heavenly twmple. Alternatively, since the parallel phrase ושערי מוצא has no suffix, the ו could be simple dittography from ושערי. If מבואי is read, the form should be taken as a combined historical/phonetic orthography (Biblical Hebrew מבוא; Mishnaic Hebrew מבוי). This seems the simplest solution.

Esther G. Chazon/Torleif Elgvin/Esther Eshel/Daniel K. Falk/Bilhah Nitzan/Elisha Qimron/Eileen M. Schuller/David Rolph Seely/Eibert J. C. Tigchelaar/Moshe Weinfeld⁦ (Hgg.), ‬Qumran Cave 4. XX. Poetical and Liturgical Texts. Part 2⁦ (‬Discoveries in the Judaean Desert 29⁦)‬⁦,‬⁦ ‬Oxford⁦ ‬1999
comment:
Notes on reading: אנה תט ס (über das א gehört ein Circellus). Only the left leg remains of the first letter; the traces fit 'alep, gimel, or he. The last three letters are spaced equidistantly at about 1 mm so that the division between words is open to interpretation (see Comments). The final letter appears to be an open samek but it is not impossible to read mem (compare the first letter of the last word on line 8 and see Notes on readings there as well as Palaeography).

אנה תט ס (über das א gehört ein Circellus). The first word might be the participle of entreaty אנא written with a he as in 2 Kgs 20:3=Isa 38:3; Jon 1:14; 4:2; Ps 116:4, 16 or, alternatively, the demonstrative particle הנה . If the last letter is samek as suggested by J. Strugnell and A. Yardeni (private communication), it would have begun a new word (the object of the verb) which followed תט (bla סליחה is one of several possibilities). The space between tet and samek is small, but this scribe sometimes does leave such a small space between words as in lines 8-9, 16. תט would be the jussive (Qal or Hip'il) of נטה in the 2nd masc. sing. "may You (God) incline/extend" (the fem. sing. , although grammatically possible, is less plausible contextually). If the last letter were read as mem, it could be taken together with the previous two letters to form a 2nd sing. imperfect or a root beginning with tet and mem such as טמן (bla טמא is less plausible if, as proposed, the subject of the verb is God).

Esther G. Chazon/Torleif Elgvin/Esther Eshel/Daniel K. Falk/Bilhah Nitzan/Elisha Qimron/Eileen M. Schuller/David Rolph Seely/Eibert J. C. Tigchelaar/Moshe Weinfeld⁦ (Hgg.), ‬Qumran Cave 4. XX. Poetical and Liturgical Texts. Part 2⁦ (‬Discoveries in the Judaean Desert 29⁦)‬⁦,‬⁦ ‬Oxford⁦ ‬1999
comment: Notes on reading: וריב לי ועדיו יעידו ב[י (für Circelli s.o.). It is possible to read the first word as וריב or יריב (see Notes on readings). In either case, the 1st sing. suffix affixed to the preposition (לי ; here the yod is certain) makes it eminently clear that the speaker is one of the contending parties. The phrase וריב לי would state "and I have a controversy"; the phrase יריב לי could be rendered "he will contend with me" or "for me", depending on how the preposition is construed (for both possibilities see BDB, 510, 936). The last word of the line can be reconstructed ב[י , "against (me)" or "for (me)", (for the less common usage of this preposition with a positive meaning see Ps 99:4 and 4Q271 I 17). ב[עדי , "on (my behalf)" is also possible; other plausible reconstructions include ב[ריבי , "in (my lawsuit)" and ב[יריבי , "against (my contenders)". These alternatives suggest four different translations of line 7: (1) "I have a controversy and his witnesses will testify against (me)", (2) "He will contend with me and his witnesses will testify against (me)", (3) "He will contend for me and his witnesses will testify on (my behalf)", (4) "I have a controversy and his witnesses will testify on (my behalf)". According to the first two translations, the contending parties are the narrator and a human opponent, reffered to in the 3rd masc. sing. ; the 2nd person is reserved for addressing God in what appears to be the speaker`s plea for divine help. The last two translations may similarly be understood in the context of a plea for the speaker`s vindication but, the figure referred to as "he", pleading on the speaker`s behalf, obviously cannot be the other party in the lawsuit. This second contending party would be some unnamed human opponent, while the "he" figure pleading on the speaker`s behalf colud be identified with God if we assume a change in the grammatical subject both here and in line 3 above. Although it is tempting to read this text in light of the prophetic rib against Israel and prayers informed by it such as 4Q381 76-77+69, there is insufficient evidence to interpret the rib motif here as a lawsuit against Israel. Rather, a better paradigm is supplied by those psalms of the individual which entreat God to champion the petitioner`s cause, strive against his adversaries and vindicate him in judgement. Compare Ps 35:1a ריבה ה את יריבי , "O Lord, strive with my adversaries"; Ps 35:23 העירה והקיצה למשפטי אלוהי ואדוני לריבי , "Wake, rouse Yourself for my cause, for my claim"; Ps 43:1a, שפטני אלוהים וריבה ריבי , "Vindicate me, O God, champion my cause" (NJPSV; compare also Ps 119:154; Lam 3:58; und weitere Qumrantexte). The nature of the rib here would then be personal strife between the speaker and his opponent(s) which he hopes will be resolved in his favour by God, to whom the text is addressed.

Esther G. Chazon/Torleif Elgvin/Esther Eshel/Daniel K. Falk/Bilhah Nitzan/Elisha Qimron/Eileen M. Schuller/David Rolph Seely/Eibert J. C. Tigchelaar/Moshe Weinfeld⁦ (Hgg.), ‬Qumran Cave 4. XX. Poetical and Liturgical Texts. Part 2⁦ (‬Discoveries in the Judaean Desert 29⁦)‬⁦,‬⁦ ‬Oxford⁦ ‬1999
comment:
Notes on reading: כדני ואין . Both readings of yod in this line are quite certain. The first letter clearly resembles a kap on PAM 42.470 (compare the kap in frg. 1 10). The space before the next letter is minimal and may indicate this is a medial kap; but such a tiny space between words is also attested in this fragment (cf. line 4). The scribe has left a blank space at the end of the line.

כדני ואין (nach diesen beiden Worten folgt freier Raum in dem Buchstaben nur vermutet werden können). If the kap is medial (see Notes on readings), it would be possible to reconstruct something like ל]כדני , "(he en)trapped me". However, in view of the legal language in previous lines, it seems quite plausible that the first word is a form of the root כדני :דין , "as my judgement " (דין spelled without yod) or ך דני ( , "yours, judge me" (דנני , with one nun lost by haplography; compare the dittography of nun in line 6). In any case, the yod at the end of this word is certain and clearly points again to a speaker who speaks in the 1st sing. A division in the text (the end of the prayer or a sub-unit) is indicated by the vacat following the word ואין , "and nothing" (the subject can no longer be determined because of the fragmentary nature of the preceding clause).

comment: Komm: "CD reads שחר. Rabin noted that the context requires שחת (perdition) on the basis of CD 6:15 (...). שחר is preferable, however, in that it is stronger than שחת. The prohibition of dealing with the sons of darkness would be expected. However, our text seems to be saying the authority of the Supervisor extends to prohibiting transactions with the children of light as well. For the use of בני שחר compare 4Q298 f1-2i:1. (Lit. ang. folgen)".
